title: Cat Oozing Skin Sore That Will Not Heal: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Discover common causes for a cat's non-healing skin sore and learn why these persistent wounds require a medium-urgency veterinary consultation.
content_html:
What Is It?

An oozing skin sore that refuses to heal can be caused by several conditions, ranging from deep bacterial infections and abscesses to more complex issues like eosinophilic granulomas or even skin cancer (squamous cell carcinoma). In cats, what looks like a simple wound on the surface can often hide a deeper pocket of infection or an underlying systemic health issue that prevents the body's natural repair process.

Is It an Emergency?

The urgency level for a non-healing, oozing sore is classified as Medium. While this is typically not an immediate life-threatening emergency like difficulty breathing or major trauma, it requires a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours. If the sore is accompanied by extreme lethargy, a loss of appetite, or a foul odor, you should seek care sooner to prevent the spread of infection into the bloodstream (sepsis).

Why a Photo Helps Triage

High-quality photos are invaluable tools for veterinary triage. A clear image allows a professional to assess the color and consistency of the discharge, the condition of the surrounding skin, and the exact location of the lesion. This helps determine whether the cat needs to be squeezed into an emergency slot or if the issue can wait for a standard appointment, and it allows the clinical team to prepare the necessary diagnostics before you even arrive.

faqs.question: Can I put antibiotic ointment on my cat's sore?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid over-the-counter ointments like Neosporin, as cats often lick them off, and some ingredients can be toxic or cause allergic reactions. Keep the area clean with a simple warm water compress until you see a vet.

faqs.question: Why is the sore not healing on its own?
faqs.answer: Sores often fail to heal due to self-trauma (licking), resistant bacterial infections, foreign bodies trapped under the skin, or underlying diseases like Feline Leukemia (FeLV) that weaken the immune system.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to treat a non-healing wound?
faqs.answer: A veterinarian will likely perform a physical exam and may recommend a 'fine needle aspirate' or a culture of the discharge to identify the specific bacteria and the correct antibiotics needed for treatment.
